label: Integrated Information Theory
definition: A theory of consciousness proposed by Giulio Tononi in 2004 that identifies conscious experience with integrated information, a quantity called phi, and claims a system is conscious to the degree that its parts form an irreducible causal whole.

body.text: Integrated Information Theory, usually abbreviated IIT, is a theory of consciousness developed by the neuroscientist Giulio Tononi beginning with a 2004 paper and refined across several versions since, with the philosopher and neuroscientist Christof Koch as its most prominent advocate. Unlike most approaches, IIT does not start from the brain and ask which mechanisms produce experience. It starts from experience itself, from a set of self-evident properties Tononi calls axioms, and asks what a physical system must be like to have them. The axioms hold that every experience exists intrinsically, is structured, is specific (it is this experience and not another), is unified (it cannot be decomposed into independent parts), and is definite (it has borders). From these, IIT derives physical postulates that any conscious substrate must satisfy.

The theory's signature quantity is phi, written with the Greek letter, a measure of integrated information. Phi quantifies how much a system's causal structure is irreducible to the structures of its parts considered separately. If you can partition a system into pieces that lose no information when severed, its phi is low or zero; the whole is just the sum of independent parts and, IIT says, there is no unified experience there. If cutting the system anywhere destroys information that the intact whole generated about itself, phi is high, the parts form a single irreducible causal entity, and that entity is conscious. The central thesis is an identity, not a correlation: a conscious experience is identical to a maximally irreducible cause-effect structure, and the amount of consciousness equals the phi of that structure. The particular quality of an experience, its qualia, is held to be the specific shape of that structure in cause-effect space.

The consequences are striking and deliberately counterintuitive. Because phi tracks integration rather than behavior or computation, IIT implies that consciousness comes in degrees and can exist wherever causal integration is high, which pushes the theory toward a qualified panpsychism: simple systems may have a small but nonzero phi and thus a dim flicker of experience. It also implies that consciousness can dissociate from intelligent behavior. A system could behave intelligently yet have low phi, and a system could have high phi yet do little. This is IIT's most provocative claim for AI: a conventional digital computer running a feedforward or weakly integrated architecture would have very low phi no matter how capable it is, because its physical causal structure, gates switching in sequence, is largely reducible. On IIT, a perfect functional simulation of a brain on such hardware would compute like a mind but not be conscious, a position Tononi and Koch summarize by saying consciousness is about being, not doing, and that you cannot get genuine experience from a mere simulation any more than a simulated black hole bends real spacetime.

The objections are serious. Scott Aaronson argued that phi can be made arbitrarily large by simple, manifestly unconscious systems such as certain error-correcting codes or grids of logic gates, which he took as a reductio of identifying consciousness with phi. Computing phi exactly is intractable for any sizable system, so the theory is hard to test directly. In 2023 a group of more than one hundred researchers publicly called IIT "pseudoscience", a charge its defenders sharply contested, arguing the critics conflated unfalsifiability with the ordinary difficulty of measurement and ignored IIT's empirical predictions about which brain regions and states carry consciousness. Critics also question whether the axioms are as self-evident as claimed, and whether deriving physics from phenomenology is a legitimate move at all, a worry that connects to the deep difficulty of the hard-problem-of-consciousness and the mind-body-problem.

For AI the stakes are unusually concrete because IIT yields a verdict, not just a worry. If IIT is even approximately right, then the path to machine consciousness runs through architecture, specifically through building systems whose physical causal structure is genuinely integrated and irreducible, not merely through scaling capability on standard hardware. It would mean that the most powerful AI systems of the present, however fluent, are likely not conscious, and that whether any future system is conscious is an objective fact about its causal organization that in principle could be measured rather than a matter we can never settle. That makes IIT one of the few theories offering an engineering criterion for machine consciousness, and one of the reasons it is taken seriously despite the controversy. It also bears directly on moral-patienthood, since a theory that grades consciousness by phi grades moral status along with it.
